task: Freeze the migration baseline and keep the three master files as the source of truth.
why_it_matters: Without a frozen baseline, redirect and content decisions drift and create SEO loss.
output_files: Same files kept versioned and reviewed
success_criteria: All stakeholders work from the same current files and no parallel spreadsheet diverges.
notes: This is the control point before making redirects or content decisions.

task: Validate that the legacy URL universe is complete before launch work starts.
why_it_matters: If legacy URLs are missing now, they will become untracked 404s after migration.
output_files: Updated urls-antiguaweb.txt if anything else appears
success_criteria: All known legacy URLs from sitemaps, exports and manual lists are present once and normalized.
notes: Add any extra sitemap or analytics export here if available.

task: Enrich the legacy list with traffic, rankings and backlinks from external SEO tools.
why_it_matters: This is how you decide what must be recreated exactly and what can be merged safely.
success_criteria: Each legacy URL has an external-value check or a deliberate 'no data' note.
notes: Use Search Console, GA4, Ahrefs, Semrush or legacy CMS exports.

task: Approve the safe redirect set: exact_match + mapped_equivalent.
why_it_matters: These are the lowest-risk 301s and can be implemented early.
success_criteria: Every exact or mapped equivalent redirect points to a semantically correct final URL.
notes: Current safe set is 3 exact_match + 97 mapped_equivalent.

task: Review every merge_to_parent redirect one by one before accepting it.
why_it_matters: A generic parent redirect is the fastest way to lose rankings on transactional URLs.
success_criteria: Each merge_to_parent row is confirmed as acceptable or moved into recreate/indexable work.
notes: Do not bulk-approve these.

task: Identify the unresolved legacy WordPress page_id URL and decide its final destination.
why_it_matters: Unidentified legacy pages should never be left as blind redirects.
success_criteria: The remaining needs_manual_decision row is either mapped exactly or marked gone with justification.
notes: Current unresolved URL is https://www.blancareal.com/?page_id=311877.

task: Review the 44 legacy URLs whose best current target exists but is still noindex.
why_it_matters: If the target stays noindex, the redirect cannot preserve SEO value.
output_files: Decision list: make indexable vs create exact equivalent
success_criteria: Each of the 44 rows has a final decision.
notes: This is the biggest fast-win bucket.

task: Implement the approved indexability changes on current targets.
why_it_matters: A good target only preserves SEO if it is indexable, canonical and crawlable.
output_files: Updated route metadata, canonicals and sitemap behavior
success_criteria: Approved targets move from noindex to indexable with correct canonical behavior.
notes: Do not open low-value thin pages just to match counts.

task: Recreate or import the legacy posts that still have SEO or link value.
why_it_matters: Informational URLs often rank independently and should not die into a blog index redirect.
success_criteria: Each valuable legacy post has a live replacement URL with equivalent topic intent.
notes: Current P1 post bucket is 16 URLs.

task: Create exact or near-exact equivalent property/project/landing pages for the highest-value transactional gaps.
why_it_matters: Generic catalogue redirects usually lose rankings for explicit property intent queries.
output_files: New landing/property URLs added to the site
success_criteria: Every approved high-intent property gap has a live, indexable destination.
notes: Current P1 create-equivalent bucket is 15 URLs.

task: Review the P2 property gaps that need a redirect-vs-rebuild decision.
why_it_matters: These are lower-confidence than P1 but still important to close before launch.
success_criteria: No P2 row remains undecided.
notes: Keep these after P1 but before final redirect freeze.

task: Handle P3 low-value gaps, including WordPress ID URLs and category archives.
why_it_matters: These usually do not justify net-new content, but they still need deliberate handling.
success_criteria: Each P3 row is marked as keep_redirect, gone, or manual lookup completed.
notes: Current P3 bucket includes property IDs and blog category archives.

task: Generate the final redirect matrix only after P1/P2/P3 decisions are closed.
why_it_matters: Redirect rules built too early bake bad assumptions into production.
success_criteria: Every legacy URL has one final migration status and one final redirect outcome.
notes: This becomes the deployable redirect source of truth.

task: Implement the 301 redirects in the real delivery layer.
why_it_matters: A spreadsheet does nothing until the platform actually serves the redirects.
success_criteria: Every approved redirect resolves as a single-hop 301 to the correct final URL.
notes: Implementation target may be Astro/Vercel/server config depending on current stack.

task: Review canonicals, hreflang and self-referencing behavior on all migration targets.
why_it_matters: Bad canonicals or hreflang can override good redirects and waste migration work.
success_criteria: Every indexable target self-canonicals correctly and multilingual alternates remain coherent.
notes: Especially important for /es /en /de /fr /it /nl.

task: Regenerate and validate the sitemap set after indexability and new content changes.
why_it_matters: Search engines need the final post-migration URL set, not the pre-migration draft.
success_criteria: Every indexable final URL is present in sitemap and excluded URLs are absent.
notes: Re-run the audit generator after content and metadata changes.

task: Update internal links so the new site points to final canonical URLs everywhere.
why_it_matters: Internal links reinforce redirects, canonicals and crawl efficiency.
success_criteria: No nav, cards, related content or footer areas point to deprecated migration targets.
notes: Avoid linking internally to URLs that only exist as redirects.

task: QA on-page SEO of recreated posts and landings.
why_it_matters: A recreated page should match search intent, metadata and indexability, not just exist.
success_criteria: Titles, H1s, meta descriptions, copy depth and structured intent are acceptable.
notes: Useful especially for high-value commercial queries.

task: Run a full redirect test from every legacy URL to the final destination.
why_it_matters: This is the final proof that nothing important breaks on go-live.
success_criteria: Each tested legacy URL returns one correct 301 chain and lands on the intended page.
notes: No redirect loops, 302s or multi-hop chains.

task: Crawl the new site to validate indexability, canonicals, noindex, 404s and orphan pages.
why_it_matters: The migration is not safe until the crawl confirms the live result.
success_criteria: No critical crawl errors remain on indexable migration targets.
notes: Use Screaming Frog or equivalent.

task: Manually check desktop and mobile on migrated templates and critical landing pages.
why_it_matters: Broken UX on key landing pages can waste recovered SEO traffic.
success_criteria: Priority pages render correctly and conversion paths work.
notes: Focus on home, properties, projects, posts, contact and migrated landings.

task: Submit final sitemaps and monitor indexing after launch.
why_it_matters: Search engines need a clean discovery path for the new URL set.
success_criteria: Final sitemap set is submitted and accepted.
notes: Keep exact submission date recorded.

task: Monitor 404s, redirected traffic, rankings and indexation after launch.
why_it_matters: Migration SEO wins or losses show up in logs and search data after release, not before.
success_criteria: Critical losses are detected quickly and corrected while redirects/content are still fresh.
notes: Check weekly for at least 6-8 weeks.

task: Freeze non-essential SEO-impacting URL changes during migration rollout.
why_it_matters: Changing slugs, noindex rules or canonicals mid-migration makes attribution impossible.
success_criteria: No untracked URL changes happen during the migration window.
notes: This is governance, not code.
